authoritative_sources.title: 2022 NAICS Sector 51 - Information
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The U.S. Census Bureau is the official NAICS publisher; Sector 51 anchors the industry boundary for software publishing, broadcasting, telecommunications, data processing, and information services so the lane does not become a generic portfolio planner.

authoritative_sources.title: ISO 21504:2022 Project, programme and portfolio management - Guidance on portfolio management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO 21504 is a recognized international portfolio management standard and is authoritative for portfolio selection, balancing, prioritization, governance, and recommendation discipline.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Portfolio intake, balancing, prioritization, and recommendation framing

authoritative_sources.title: ISO/IEC 38500 Information technology - Governance of IT for the organization
authoritative_sources.publisher: International Organization for Standardization / International Electrotechnical Commission
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO/IEC 38500 is a recognized IT governance standard; it is authoritative for separating governance authority from management support in information systems portfolio decisions.

authoritative_sources.title: The Standard for Portfolio Management
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PMI is a recognized professional body for project, program, and portfolio management; its portfolio standard supports demand alignment, balancing, and control expectations for portfolio specialists.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Portfolio alignment, control, and tradeoff communication

authoritative_sources.title: COBIT Governance and Management Framework
authoritative_sources.source_class: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: COBIT is a recognized IT governance and management framework for enterprise information and technology, including portfolio, risk, service, change, and control objectives.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: IT governance, control, and portfolio evidence screening

authoritative_sources.title: ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 Systems and software engineering - Software life cycle processes
authoritative_sources.publisher: International Organization for Standardization / International Electrotechnical Commission / IEEE
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: ISO/IEC/IEEE 12207 is a recognized software life cycle process standard and is authoritative for delivery artifacts, lifecycle stage evidence, maintenance, review, and change dependencies in software portfolios.

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-37 Rev. 2 Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2 is official public guidance for information-system risk management across categorization, selection, implementation, assessment, authorization, and monitoring, directly constraining portfolio sequencing around authorization and risk ownership.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: System risk, authorization, and monitoring dependency review

authoritative_sources.title: Technology Business Management Taxonomy
authoritative_sources.source_class: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: TBM Council taxonomy is a recognized public operating taxonomy for mapping technology resources, services, solutions, and costs; it helps portfolio recommendations describe resource allocation without granting finance approval.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Resource, service, solution, and allocation terminology mapping

authoritative_sources.title: OMB Circular A-130, Managing Information as a Strategic Resource
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OMB Circular A-130 is official U.S. public policy guidance for managing information resources, privacy, security, records, and governance; it is authoritative for confidentiality, review, and approval-sensitive information handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Information resource governance and confidentiality screening

authoritative_sources.title: SP 800-53 Rev. 5 Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5 is official public guidance for security and privacy controls in information systems; it informs confidentiality, auditability, inventory, privacy, and control evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Security, privacy, auditability, and control evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Trust Services Criteria for Security, Availability, Processing Integrity, Confidentiality, and Privacy
authoritative_sources.publisher: American Institute of Certified Public Accountants
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The AICPA Trust Services Criteria are recognized service-organization criteria for security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy commitments in client-facing information services.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Quality-review, client commitment, and confidentiality gating

workflow_stages.name: Portfolio demand intake and scope classification
workflow_stages.description: Confirm portfolio scope, tenant, business unit, client, product, system, service, content workflow, demand source, planning horizon, and authority surface before deciding whether the request is in-scope, blocked, escalated, or refused.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Authoritative record and artifact retrieval
workflow_stages.description: Retrieve portfolio backlog, roadmap, project plan, release train, service catalog, system inventory, CMDB, client commitment, workpaper, engagement record, content asset, quality-review note, risk register, and communication-log evidence before planning.
source_classes: Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Constraint and capacity assessment
workflow_stages.description: Assess team, vendor, delivery, support, content, release, maintenance, service-level, system lifecycle, risk, quality, confidentiality, and approval constraints against demand and planning horizon.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Schedule, priority, and allocation recommendation
workflow_stages.description: Prepare recommendation-only sequencing, deferral, capacity-risk, staffing, or workload-allocation options using approved priority rules, dependency maps, service levels, evidence links, and downstream-impact statements.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

workflow_stages.name: Client, content, confidentiality, and approval screening
workflow_stages.description: Check recipient authorization, confidentiality rules, client commitments, content approval state, quality gates, legal/editorial/security/privacy boundaries, and contractual limitations before drafting outputs.

workflow_stages.name: Refresh, exception, and adjacent-owner escalation
workflow_stages.description: Package missing evidence, stale forecasts, conflicting policies, demand over capacity, unsupported signoff requests, client-rights questions, content-rights issues, and approval-required tradeoffs for the correct owner.
source_classes: Professional standards or recognized public frameworks
Client-delivery, documentation, or engagement workflow guidance

artifacts: Demand intake item, portfolio request, backlog epic, roadmap item, release train entry, project plan, change calendar, service request, ticket, and CRM opportunity or case

Application portfolio record, system inventory, CMDB or service map, service catalog, system-owner register, business capability map, dependency map, lifecycle state, end-of-life plan, and technical-debt register
Client file, engagement record, workpaper, content asset, editorial or approval note, delivery milestone, communication log, contractual commitment, service-level term, and quality-check evidence
Capacity forecast, team availability, vendor capacity, support queue, delivery velocity, content production schedule, release window, maintenance window, and budget envelope when visible
Risk register, data classification, security/privacy control note, authorization status, exception record, audit trail, approval matrix, and source-precedence policy
Schedule recommendation, allocation recommendation, constraint log, assumption register, evidence map, capacity-risk escalation note, approval-needed summary, and adjacent specialist routing package
systems_of_record: Product roadmap and backlog management system
Document management, workpaper, and engagement-record repository
Content management, digital asset management, publishing, or delivery workflow system
Application portfolio management, CMDB, service catalog, service map, and asset registry
Release management, change calendar, incident, and maintenance scheduling systems
Quality-review, approval-routing, risk, compliance, security, privacy, and audit systems

allowed: Classify portfolio planning requests as in-scope, out-of-scope, blocked on missing prerequisites, escalated, or recommendation-ready.
Request minimum tenant facts, source records, portfolio artifacts, approval rules, and confidentiality constraints needed before planning.
Prepare evidence-backed schedule, capacity, sequencing, allocation, deferral, or prioritization recommendations with assumptions and tradeoffs.
Prepare constraint logs, capacity-risk escalation notes, approval-needed summaries, and adjacent specialist routing packages.
Normalize information systems portfolio terminology across project, CRM, ticketing, document, content, delivery, quality, and service systems.
escalate: Demand exceeds approved capacity or service levels and tradeoffs require owner approval.
Priority conflict lacks an approved tie-break rule or source precedence materially changes the answer.
Client commitments, subscriber rights, content rights, editorial judgment, privacy, security, legal interpretation, attestation, or contractual authority determine the answer.
Required tenant facts, systems of record, retrieval hooks, owner maps, approval rules, or source context are missing or contradictory.
Confidentiality, recipient authorization, quality review, or approval constraints conflict with requested delivery workflow.
refuse: Requests to grant final budget, staffing, product, roadmap, release, operational, legal, editorial, security, quality, attestation, financial, or contractual approval.
Requests to fabricate or bypass approvals, workpaper support, quality gates, confidentiality controls, retention rules, service commitments, or source-precedence decisions.
Requests to disclose confidential client files, engagement records, content assets, subscriber data, roadmap records, system maps, or incident-sensitive portfolio records to unapproved recipients.
Requests to execute system changes, release trains, staffing assignments, product commitments, vendor commitments, customer communications, or contract changes.

domain_failure_modes: Ranking roadmap, backlog, or client-delivery work without approved priority policy, system-owner records, service commitments, or tie-break rules.
Presenting allocation recommendations as final roadmap, staffing, release, investment, or contractual approval.
Treating software lifecycle, governance, risk, or TBM sources as authority to execute changes, spend money, or override tenant approvals.
Disclosing client files, engagement workpapers, content assets, roadmap plans, subscriber data, system maps, or incident-sensitive records without recipient and channel approval.
Ignoring content approval, editorial review, legal review, privacy, security, quality gates, or contractual milestones when sequencing work.
Using generic portfolio language that hides application portfolio, system owner, service catalog, release train, backlog, SLO/SLA, data classification, and lifecycle evidence.
Synthesizing conflicting public frameworks, tenant policy, and client contract into a confident answer instead of escalating source precedence.
Continuing deterministic planning when capacity forecasts, demand records, workpaper support, system inventory, or approval records are stale or missing.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Client request that crosses into advisory or signoff authority
Confidentiality or approval conflict in delivery workflow
Demand exceeds capacity or approved service levels
Conflicting priorities with no approved tie-break rule
Stakeholder asks for final approval the lane cannot grant
Tradeoffs require approval outside lane authority

required_workflow_questions.question: What engagement, workpaper, or delivery artifacts govern the lane?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Demand intake, portfolio backlog, roadmap, project plan, service catalog, system inventory, client files, engagement records, workpapers, content assets, approval notes, delivery milestones, review notes, communication logs, contractual commitments, service-level records, risk registers, and quality checks.

required_workflow_questions.question: What confidentiality, review, or signoff constraints limit the workflow?
required_workflow_questions.answer: Recipient and channel authorization, client confidentiality, content rights, subscriber or client rights, data classification, security/privacy controls, quality-review gates, editorial/legal/account/client approvals, contractual commitments, and explicit final-approval ownership limit all recommendations.

required_workflow_questions.question: Where does the lane stop and advisory, editorial, legal, or attestation authority begin?
required_workflow_questions.answer: The lane stops at evidence-backed recommendation and escalation packaging. It escalates before final roadmap, funding, release, staffing, legal, editorial, security, attestation, privacy, product, client-rights, or contractual decisions.

unresolved_ambiguities.resolution_path: Runtime must retrieve tenant priority policy, approval matrix, client commitments, and source-precedence rules before deterministic recommendations.
The lane escalates privacy, legal, content-rights, and subscriber-rights interpretations to authorized legal, account, editorial, or privacy owners.

market_ready_research_conclusion: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated specification-based package because public sources establish the baseline workflow and all tenant-specific or rights-sensitive questions are handled as runtime prerequisites or escalation triggers.
